audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man phoning to inquire about a job vacancy.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good evening, King's restaurant.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Good evening.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm ringing about the job.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I understand you have vacant.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The job vacancy is at a restaurant.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, a restaurant has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good evening, King's restaurant.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Good evening.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm ringing about the job.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I understand you have vacant.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'd like to find out a few more details if I may.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Can I take your name?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's Peter Chin.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, Peter.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, if you want to ask about the job, and then if we're both still interested, we could arrange for you to come for an interview.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Great, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm afraid I missed the advert for the job, but heard about it from a friend.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's no problem at all.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What would you like to know?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, uh, what sort of work is it?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Washing up?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's answering the phone.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, right, fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And not waiting at table.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That would be good, and how many nights a week would it be?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, we're really only busy at the weekend.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, two nights?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Three actually, so it would work out at 12 hours a week.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That would be fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It wouldn't interfere with my studies.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Are you at the university?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, 1st year physics student.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Um. And because I'm not an EU national, would I need a work permit?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, you would.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Just get your tutor to sign it.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And that wouldn't be a problem if I were to get the job.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. Where exactly is the restaurant?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, we have 2 branches.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The one we're recruiting for is in Hillsdunne Road.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. I don't know that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. How do you spell it please?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's HILLSDUNNE Road.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Got that, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I- is it near a bus stop?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, the nearest one would probably be just beside the library.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh yes, I know it.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That would be fine for me.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And could I ask about the pay?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We're offering £4.45 an hour.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's very good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: My last job was £3.95 an hour.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We feel it's pretty good, and we also offer some good fringe benefits.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, we give you a free dinner, so you eat well.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right, better than hostel food.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We certainly hope so.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And we also offer extra pay for working on national holidays.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that's a really good perk, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, we think so.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And then because of the difficulties of getting public transport,
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: if you're working after 11 o'clock, we drive you home.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that's good to know.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, we'd certainly be interested in inviting you for an interview, if you're still interested.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh yes, certainly.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Could I just also ask what qualities you're looking for?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, for this particular job, we want a clear voice, which you obviously do have.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And you must be able to think quickly, you know.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I hope I'd...
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So, when could you come in for an interview?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We're actually quite quiet tonight.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Sorry, I couldn't come tonight, or tomorrow, I'm afraid.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Thursday's OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That'd be 22nd of October.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Fine, after 5 pm.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Would 6 o'clock be OK?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Perfect.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And could you bring along the names of two referees?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: No problem.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Good, I look forward to seeing you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, uh, by the way, who should I ask for?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sorry, my name is Samira Manuja.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Can you spell that please?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: MANUJA.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, I've got that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thanks very much.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Look forward to seeing you...
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Sports World's New Branch in Bradcaster

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a radio program about the opening of a new local sports shop.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we go to Jane, who is going to tell us about what's happening in town this weekend.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Right, thanks Andrew.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And now on to what's new, and do we really need yet another sports shop in Bradcaster?
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Well, most of you probably know Sports World, the branch of a Danish sports goods company that opened a few years ago.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: It's attracted a lot of custom.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And so the company has now decided to open another branch in the area.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: It's going to be in the shopping center to the west of Bradcaster.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: So that will be good news for all of you who've found the original shop in the north of the town hard to get to.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I was invited to a special preview, and I can promise you, this is the ultimate in sports retailing.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: The whole place has been given a new minimalist look with the company's signature colors of black and red.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: The first three floors have a huge range of sports clothing as well as equipment.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And on the top floor, there's a café and a book and DVD section.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: You'll find all the well-known names, as well as some less well-known ones.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: If they haven't got exactly what you want in stock, they promise to get it for you in 10 days, unlike the other store, where it can take up to 14 days.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: They cover all the major sports, including football, tennis, and swimming.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: But they particularly focus on running, and they claim to have the widest range of equipment in the country.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: As well as that, a whole section of the third floor is devoted to sports bags, including the latest designs from the States.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: If you can't find what you want here, it doesn't exist.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the program, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The shop will be open from 9:00 am this Saturday, and if you go along to the opening, then you'll have the chance to meet the national 400 meters running champion Paul King.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Who's coming along to open the shop, and he will be staying around until about midday to chat to any fans who want to meet him and sign autographs.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Then there will be a whole range of special attractions all weekend.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: There will be free tickets for local sporting events for the first 50 customers.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And also a special competition open to all.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Just answer 15 out of 20 sports questions correctly to win a signed copy of Paul King's DVD 'Spring Tips', while the first person to get all the questions correct gets a year's free membership of the Bradcaster Gym.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: All entrants will receive a special sports calendar, with details of all Bradcaster fixtures in the coming year.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: One of the special opening offers is a fitness test, a complete review of your cardiac fitness and muscle tone, actually done in the shop by qualified staff.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: This would normally cost £30 but is available at half price for this month only.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: There are only a limited number of places available for this.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: So to make a booking, phone 5 6 0 3 4 1.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: In addition, if you open an account, you get lots more special offers.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Including the chance to try out equipment at special open evenings.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: But they particularly focus on running, and they claim to have the widest range of equipment in the country.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: As well as that, a whole section of the third floor is devoted to sports bags, including the latest designs from the States.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: If you can't find what you want here, it doesn't exist.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the program, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The shop will be open from 9:00 am this Saturday, and if you go along to the opening, then you'll have the chance to meet the national 400 meters running champion Paul King.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: Who's coming along to open the shop, and he will be staying around until about midday to chat to any fans who want to meet him and sign autographs.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: Then there will be a whole range of special attractions all weekend.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: There will be free tickets for local sporting events for the first 50 customers.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: And also a special competition open to all.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: Just answer 15 out of 20 sports questions correctly to win a signed copy of Paul King's DVD 'Spring Tips', while the first person to get all the questions correct gets a year's free membership of the Bradcaster Gym.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: All entrants will receive a special sports calendar, with details of all Bradcaster fixtures in the coming year.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: One of the special opening offers is a fitness test, a complete review of your cardiac fitness and muscle tone, actually done in the shop by qualified staff.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: This would normally cost £30 but is available at half price for this month only.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: There are only a limited number of places available for this.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: So to make a booking, phone 5 6 0 3 4 1.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: In addition, if you open an account, you get lots more special offers.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: Including the chance to try out equipment at special open evenings.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: a new <input> of an international sports goods company
located in the shopping centre to the <input> of Bradcaster
has sports <input> and equipment on floors 1 - 3
can get you any item within <input> days
shop specialises in equipment for <input>
has a special section which just sells <input>
body.title: SPORTS WORLD

items.title: A champion athlete will be in the shop
The first person to answer 20 quiz questions correctly will win

body.title: Which TWO pieces of information does the speaker give about the fitness test?
options: You need to reserve a place.
It is free to account holders.
You get advice on how to improve your health.
It takes place in a special clinic.

test_text.title: Discussion on the Value of English for Academic Purposes Course

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Two overseas students called Spiros and Hiroko have just finished the first semester of their university course.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: They are discussing with their English language teacher,
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: how they coped with the course.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Before we start, Spiros and Hiroko, thanks for coming in today to talk about your recent study experiences, and congratulations to you both in doing so well in your first semester exams.
audioscript.speaker: TEACHER

audioscript.text: I'd like to discuss with you the value of the English for Academic Purposes course you did here last year before starting your university course.
audioscript.speaker: TEACHER

audioscript.text: Uh. Spiros, if I could start with you.
audioscript.speaker: TEACHER

audioscript.text: What parts of the program have now proved to be particularly valuable to you?
audioscript.speaker: TEACHER

audioscript.text: Um. I think that having to do a seminar presentation really helped me.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Uh. For example, a couple of weeks ago in our marketing subject, when it was my turn to give a presentation, I felt quite confident.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Of course, I was still nervous, but because I had done one before, I knew what to expect.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Uh. Also I know I was well prepared, and I had practiced my timing.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: In fact, I think that in relation to some of the other people in my group.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: I did quite a good job, uh, because my overall style was quite professional.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: What about you, Hiroko?
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Hmm, that's interesting.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: In my group, I was really surprised by the way the students did their presentations.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: They just read their notes aloud.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Can you believe that?
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: They didn't worry about their presentation style or keeping eye contact with their audience.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: And I remember that these things were really stressed to us in the course here.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: So how did you approach your presentation, Hiroko?
audioscript.speaker: TEACHER

audioscript.text: Well, to speak frankly, I read my notes too.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: At the time, it was a relief to do it this way, but actually, when I had finished, I didn't feel any real sense of satisfaction.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: I didn't feel positive about the experience at all.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: That's a pity.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: You know, although I was pleased with my presentation, I am not so pleased with my actual performance right now in the tutorials.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: During the whole semester, I've not said anything in our tutorial discussions.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Not a word.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Spiros, why is that?
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Do the other students talk too much?
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: It's partly that, but it's mostly because I have had no confidence to speak out.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Their style of speaking is so different.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: It's not the style we were used to during the course.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: They use so many colloquialisms.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: They're not very polite.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: And sometimes there seems to be no order in their discussion.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Also they are very familiar with each other, so because they know each other's habits, they can let each other into the discussion.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: You're right, Spiros.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: I've experienced that too.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: For most of this semester, I've said absolutely nothing in tutorials.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: But recently, I've been trying to speak up more, and I just jump in.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: And I've noticed an interesting thing.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: I've noticed that if they thought my point was interesting or new, then the next time they actually asked for my opinion.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: And then it was much easier for me to be part of the discussion.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Oh, that's great, Hiroko.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: I hope that happens for me next semester.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: I'll have to work hard to find some interesting points.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: What helped you to find these ideas?
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: I think that one thing that helped me with this was the reading.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: I've had to do so much reading this semester just to help me make sense of the lectures.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: At first, I couldn't understand what the lecturers were talking about.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: So, I had to turn to the books and journals.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Every night, I read for hours, using the lists of references that were given, and I made pages of notes.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: At breakfast, I read and read my notes again.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: This habit has helped me to follow the ideas in the lectures.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: And it's also given me some ideas to use in the tutorials.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: But I did so much reading anyway, I don't think there's any time left over for anything extra.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: My reading speed is still quite slow, though I'm much better at dealing with vocabulary than I used to be.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: What else do you think we could add to the course program to help with this reading problem?
audioscript.speaker: TEACHER

audioscript.text: Hmm, uh. There's not really anything because it's my problem.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: I remember we were given long articles to read.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: We didn't like that, but now I realize that reading those long articles was good preparation for the things I need to read now.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Also, uh, in class, we regularly had speed reading tasks to do, and we kept a record of our reading speed, so the teachers were encouraging us to work on that.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: That's true, Spiros, but what we read could have been different.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Sometimes in the English class, I felt frustrated when I had to read articles about the environment or health or education.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Because I wanted to concentrate on my own field, but we didn't read anything about engineering.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: So I think I wasted some time learning vocabulary I didn't need.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Hmm. But surely the strategies you were taught for dealing with that vocabulary were helpful.
audioscript.speaker: TEACHER

audioscript.text: Yes, but psychologically speaking, I would have felt much better working on reading from my own field.
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Hmm. What do you think, Spiros?
audioscript.speaker: HIROKO

audioscript.text: Oh, I agree.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: That would have helped my confidence too, and I would have been more motivated.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: Uh. It was good though that we could work on our own topics when we wrote the research assignments.
audioscript.speaker: SPIROS

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: TEACHER

audioscript.text: Let's move on to writing now...
audioscript.speaker: TEACHER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: One reason why Spiros felt happy about his marketing presentation was that
options: the presentation was the best in his group.

items.title: What surprised Hiroko about the other students' presentations?
options: They didn't look at the audience enough.

items.title: After she gave her presentation, Hiroko felt
How does Spiros feel about his performance in tutorials?
Why can the other students participate so easily in discussions?
options: They are polite to each other.
They agree to take turns in speaking.

items.title: Why is Hiroko feeling more positive about tutorials now?
options: She finds the other students' opinions more interesting.
She is making more of a contribution.
The tutor includes her in the discussion.

items.title: To help her understand lectures, Hiroko
options: had extra tutorials with her lecturers.
borrowed lecture notes from other students.

items.title: What does Spiros think of his reading skills?
options: He reads faster than he used to.
It still takes him a long time to read.
He tends to struggle with new vocabulary.

items.title: Hiroko thinks that in the reading classes the students should
options: read more in their own subject areas.

test_text.title: Reasons for Mass Strandings of Whales and Dolphins

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture about a problem related to the behavior of certain sea creatures.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good afternoon everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, with some of you about to go out on fieldwork, it's timely that in this afternoon's session, I'll be sharing some ideas about the reasons why groups of whales and dolphins sometimes swim ashore from the sea,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: right onto the beach, and most often die in what are known as 'mass strandings'.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unfortunately,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: this type of event is a frequent occurrence in some of the locations that you'll be traveling to, where sometimes the tide goes out suddenly, confusing the animals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, there are many other theories about the causes of mass strandings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The first is that the behavior is linked to parasites.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's often found that stranded animals were infested with large numbers of parasites.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For instance, a type of worm is commonly found in the ears of dead whales.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Since marine animals rely heavily on their hearing to navigate,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: this type of infestation has the potential to be very harmful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another theory is related to toxins or poisons.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These have also been found to contribute to the death of many marine animals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many toxins, as I'm sure you're aware, originate from plants or animals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The whale ingests these toxins in its normal feeding behavior, but whether these poisons directly or indirectly lead to stranding and death seems to depend upon the toxin involved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 1988, for example, 14 humpback whales examined after stranding along the beaches of Cape Cod were found to have been poisoned after eating tuna that contained saxitoxin.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The same toxin that can be fatal in humans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Alternatively, it has also been suggested that some animals strand accidentally by following their prey ashore in the confusion of the chase, in 1995 David Thurston monitored pilot whales that beached after following squid ashore.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, this idea does not seem to hold true for the majority of mass strandings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because examination of the animals' stomach contents revealed that most had not been feeding as they stranded.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are also some new theories which link strandings to humans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A growing concern is that loud noises in the ocean cause strandings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Noises such as those caused by military exercises are of particular concern, and have been pinpointed as the cause of some strandings of late.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of these, a mass stranding of whales in 2000 in the Bahamas coincided closely with experiments using a new submarine detection system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There were several factors that made this stranding stand out as different from previous strandings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This led researchers to look for a new cause.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For one, all the stranded animals were healthy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In addition, the animals were spread out along 38 km of coast.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Whereas it's more common for the animals to be found in a group when mass strandings occur.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A final theory is related to group behavior, and suggests that sea mammals cannot distinguish between sick and healthy leaders, and will follow sick leaders.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Even to an inevitable death.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is a particularly interesting theory, since the whales that are thought to be most social, the toothed whales,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: are the group that strand the most frequently.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The theory is also supported by evidence from a dolphin stranding in 1994.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Examination of the dead animals revealed that apart from the leader.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All the others had been healthy at the time of their death.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Without one consistent theory, however, it is very hard for us to do anything about this phenomenon, except to assist animals where and when we can.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Stranding networks have been established around the world to aid in rescuing animals and collecting samples from those that could not be helped.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I recommend John Connor's Marine Mammals Ashore.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As an excellent starting point, if you're interested in finding out more about these networks or establishing one yourself.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a lecture about a problem related to the behavior of certain sea creatures.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Good afternoon everyone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, with some of you about to go out on fieldwork, it's timely that in this afternoon's session, I'll be sharing some ideas about the reasons why groups of whales and dolphins sometimes swim ashore from the sea,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: right onto the beach, and most often die in what are known as 'mass strandings'.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Unfortunately,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: this type of event is a frequent occurrence in some of the locations that you'll be traveling to, where sometimes the tide goes out suddenly, confusing the animals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, there are many other theories about the causes of mass strandings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The first is that the behavior is linked to parasites.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's often found that stranded animals were infested with large numbers of parasites.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For instance, a type of worm is commonly found in the ears of dead whales.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Since marine animals rely heavily on their hearing to navigate,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: this type of infestation has the potential to be very harmful.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another theory is related to toxins or poisons.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These have also been found to contribute to the death of many marine animals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many toxins, as I'm sure you're aware, originate from plants or animals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The whale ingests these toxins in its normal feeding behavior, but whether these poisons directly or indirectly lead to stranding and death seems to depend upon the toxin involved.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In 1988, for example, 14 humpback whales examined after stranding along the beaches of Cape Cod were found to have been poisoned after eating tuna that contained saxitoxin.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The same toxin that can be fatal in humans.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Alternatively, it has also been suggested that some animals strand accidentally by following their prey ashore in the confusion of the chase, in 1995 David Thurston monitored pilot whales that beached after following squid ashore.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, this idea does not seem to hold true for the majority of mass strandings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because examination of the animals' stomach contents revealed that most had not been feeding as they stranded.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are also some new theories which link strandings to humans.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A growing concern is that loud noises in the ocean cause strandings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Noises such as those caused by military exercises are of particular concern, and have been pinpointed as the cause of some strandings of late.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of these, a mass stranding of whales in 2000 in the Bahamas coincided closely with experiments using a new submarine detection system.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There were several factors that made this stranding stand out as different from previous strandings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This led researchers to look for a new cause.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For one, all the stranded animals were healthy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In addition, the animals were spread out along 38 km of coast.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Whereas it's more common for the animals to be found in a group when mass strandings occur.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A final theory is related to group behavior, and suggests that sea mammals cannot distinguish between sick and healthy leaders, and will follow sick leaders.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Even to an inevitable death.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is a particularly interesting theory, since the whales that are thought to be most social, the toothed whales,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: are the group that strand the most frequently.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The theory is also supported by evidence from a dolphin stranding in 1994.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Examination of the dead animals revealed that apart from the leader.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All the others had been healthy at the time of their death.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Without one consistent theory, however, it is very hard for us to do anything about this phenomenon, except to assist animals where and when we can.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Stranding networks have been established around the world to aid in rescuing animals and collecting samples from those that could not be helped.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I recommend John Connor's Marine Mammals Ashore.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As an excellent starting point, if you're interested in finding out more about these networks or establishing one yourself.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
